cases.title: Colquitt v. Houston Metro Presbytery
cases.synopsis: TE Colquitt complained that Houston Metro Presbytery's Judicial Commission erred in ruling the Gleason Complaint out of order. The SJC answered Colquitt's Complaint by reference to its ruling in Case 2024-02 (Gleason v. Houston Metro Presbytery), finding that the BCO 43 complaint process is not a proper avenue for accusations, and denied the complaint 21-0-0.

cases.title: Mueller v. South Florida Presbytery
cases.synopsis: RE Mueller complained that the Redlands Community Church Session erred in allowing Alcoholics Anonymous to use church facilities for weekly meetings, ultimately reaching the SJC after the Session's 14-month failure to consider the complaint and South Florida Presbytery's denial. The SJC denied the complaint, finding AA's use of church property was a matter of Session discretion under BCO 12-5 that did not rise to clear constitutional error, while expressing concern about the Session's lengthy failure to act on the original complaint.

cases.title: Harris v. Wisconsin Presbytery
cases.synopsis: RE Harris, as a Presbytery prosecutor, complained that Wisconsin Presbytery's Judicial Commission erred in dismissing both indictments against a pastor for alleged pastoral care failures. The SJC denied the complaint 18-4-0, finding the JC's dismissal of Indictment 1 under BCO 32-20 (passage of time) was within its discretion despite noting some troubling reasoning by JC members, and that the dismissal of Indictment 2 was authorized because the charge arose from a private offense never brought through proper process; four members dissented as to Indictment 1, arguing the JC clearly erred by conducting a de facto trial with only the accused's unsworn testimony.

cases.title: DeWitt v. Nashville Presbytery
cases.synopsis: TE DeWitt requested withdrawal of his appeal against Nashville Presbytery on November 21, 2024. The SJC dismissed the case per the Appellant's request.

cases.title: Benyola v. Central Florida Presbytery
cases.synopsis: Mr. Benyola filed a complaint against Central Florida Presbytery but lacked standing as a non-member of any PCA congregation (BCO 43-1). The SJC dismissed the complaint as administratively out of order, controlled by its prior decisions in Cases 2022-17, 18, and 19 involving the same complainant, voted 21-0-0 with 2 absent and 1 disqualified.
